name: Vegvisir
description: The Vegvisir, also known as the runic compass or Viking compass, is an Icelandic magical stave intended to help the bearer find their way through rough weather. The symbol is composed of eight rune-like staves emanating from a central point, each with a unique shape and meaning. It is believed that carrying or possessing the Vegvisir would prevent the bearer from getting lost, whether at sea or on land, ensuring they always find their way home. The Vegvisir's power lies not just in physical navigation, but also in providing spiritual guidance and protection against life's unpredictable storms. While the exact origins of the Vegvisir are shrouded in mystery, it is primarily associated with Icelandic magic and seafaring traditions. The symbol's appearance in the Huld Manuscript suggests its use was tied to practical navigation and safeguarding travelers. The Vegvisir is a potent symbol of guidance, protection, and the ability to navigate life's uncertainties with courage and determination. It embodies the Norse spirit of exploration and resilience, reminding us that even in the darkest of times, we can find our way.
subtitle: The Viking Compass: Guidance and Protection
origin: Icelandic magical tradition, possibly derived from earlier Norse symbols.
currentLocation: Unknown, depicted in various forms worldwide.

keyMyths.title: Finding Your Way
keyMyths.description: The Vegvisir is believed to help the bearer find their way in any weather, ensuring they never get lost. It provides both physical and spiritual guidance through difficult journeys.

keyMyths.title: Protection at Sea
keyMyths.description: Sailors often used the Vegvisir to protect themselves from getting lost at sea, a common fear in Norse culture. The symbol was thought to provide safe passage and a return home.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: An Icelandic collection of magical staves and symbols, including the Vegvisir.
sources.text: An Icelandic collection of magical staves and symbols, including the Vegvisir.
